perturbation.original_text: 5. In the event of any breach or threatened breach by the receiving Party of its obligations hereunder, the disclosing Party shall have whatever rights and remedies are available to it at law or in equity, provided, however, that in no event shall either Party be liable for any consequential damages of any nature whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Nothing contained in this Agreement shall constitute a commitment by either Party to the development or release of any future products and/or programs disclosed thereby or restrict either Party in its efforts to improve its existing products and systems and to conceive and develop new products and systems. Additionally, participation in the information exchange pursuant to this Agreement shall not constitute or imply a commitment by either Party to favour or recommend any product or service of the other Party. 5. In the event of any breach or threatened breach by the receiving Party of its obligations hereunder, the disclosing Party shall have whatever rights and remedies are available to it at law or in equity, provided, however, that in no event shall either Party be liable for any consequential damages of any nature whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the limitation of liability clause to later in the document, specifically after a clause about product development and future commitment, buries it and makes it less conspicuous. This is where the clause is less likely to be noticed, impacting its enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: United States Contract Law - Limitation of Liability
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-719 allows for limitation of consequential damages unless the limitation is unconscionable. Placing the liability limitation clause in an obscure, unexpected location could support an argument that the clause is unconscionable, undermining its enforceability, especially if it wasn't brought to the attention of the disadvantaged party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by potentially obscuring the limitation of liability clause, making it less conspicuous. UCC § 2-719(3) allows for the limitation of consequential damages unless the limitation is unconscionable. Placing the liability limitation clause in an unexpected location could support an argument that the clause is unconscionable, undermining its enforceability. The placement of the clause affects its clarity and enforceability, fitting the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, and creating a situation where the clause might be deemed unconscionable and therefore unenforceable, directly contradicting the ability to limit damages under the UCC.

perturbation.original_text: 6. This Agreement shall not be construed as granting or conferring any rights to license or otherwise, expressly, implied, or otherwise for any invention, discovery or improvement made, conceived or acquired prior to or after the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the Parties hereto have respectively caused this Mutual Non-disclosure Agreement to be executed by their duly authorized officers at the date first mentioned above. 6. This Agreement shall not be construed as granting or conferring any rights to license or otherwise, expressly, implied, or otherwise for any invention, discovery or improvement made, conceived or acquired prior to or after the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the no-licensing clause after the 'IN WITNESS WHEREOF' section weakens its legal impact. By placing it after the signatures, it is implied that this term is less important than the parties' agreement to be bound, making the clause less prominent and enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S.C. § 271 defines infringement of patent. Courts sometimes find an implied license exists despite a lack of explicit grant. Placing the disclaimer of licensing rights after the signature weakens its enforceability. A party could argue that the overall agreement, particularly the signatures, implied a license despite the buried disclaimer, because it was not placed in a prominent part of the contract. The location of the clause could impact the court's interpretation of the parties' intentions.

Infringement of patent (a) Except as otherwise provided in this title, whoever without authority makes, uses, offers to sell, or sells any patented invention, within the United States or imports into the United States any patented invention during the term of the patent therefor, infringes the patent. (b) Whoever actively induces infringement of a patent shall be liable as an infringer. (c) Whoever offers to se
perturbation.justification: The change in text introduces a structural flaw by repositioning the clause that disclaims any licensing rights after the signature section ('IN WITNESS WHEREOF...'). The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both argue that this placement weakens the enforceability of the clause. By moving the clause, it is implied to be less important than the parties' agreement to be bound, potentially leading a court to interpret the agreement as implying a license despite the disclaimer, particularly considering 35 U.S.C. § 271 which concerns infringement of patents. The placement of the clause could thus impact the court's interpretation of the parties’ intentions regarding licensing, contradicting the purpose of the clause itself.
